mappings.rationaleAI: Metallurgy is the exact subject of the B division — covering mineral processing (B.1), all branches of extractive metallurgy including pyrometallurgy, electrometallurgy, and hydrometallurgy (B.2), and physical metallurgy (B.3).

Metal as the substrate of metallurgical production processes covers the physical and chemical properties that govern physical metallurgy (B.3), including heat treatment, surface treatment, and mechanical working.

Industrial kilns and furnaces — including those for ceramics firing, glass production, and metallurgical smelting — are the primary equipment for heat-treating operations (A.5.a) and pyrometallurgical processes (B.2.a).

Vulcanization (sulfur cross-linking) is a chemical processing operation (A.1.b: polymerization/coagulation) that transforms raw latex, and rubber molding (injection/compression) relates to casting and molding nonmetals (A.2.a.ii).

Silver refining by pyrometallurgical methods (B.2.a.iv) and electrorefining (B.2.b.iii) are directly noted in the extractive metallurgy outline; the article covers silver's production as a byproduct of other metal refining.

Alloy production involves heat treatment (B.3.f — annealing, quenching, tempering to achieve target microstructures) and property modification (A.5); the article covers how alloying and heat treatment alter microstructure and mechanical properties.

Glass production involves casting and molding molten silicate material (A.2.a.ii — casting/molding nonmetals) and property modification through controlled quenching and annealing; the article covers soda-lime, borosilicate, and specialty glass production.

Manufacturing provides an overview of industrial production processes — fabrication, assembly, forming, and finishing across industries — that encompasses the scope of section A (materials processing operations).

Steel production maps to converting processes (B.2.a.iii: basic oxygen steelmaking), heat treatment for microstructural control (B.3.f), and welding (B.4: weldability and applications of steel structures).

Iron smelting via blast furnace (B.2.a.ii — smelting) and direct reduction, plus its conversion to steel (B.2.a.iii), are core pyrometallurgical operations directly addressed in the extractive metallurgy section.
Copper production exemplifies converting to blister copper (B.2.a.iii), pyrometallurgical refining (B.2.a.iv), and electrorefining (B.2.b.iii)—the outline explicitly names copper in all three nodes.

Cryogenic temperature processing (A.5.a.iv) for materials treatment connects to refrigeration technology; industrial refrigeration also underpins cold-storage warehousing (C.4), one of the priority uncovered paths.

Plastics are produced by chemical processing including polymerization (A.1.b) and shaped by injection molding, extrusion, and compression (A.2.a.ii — casting and molding nonmetals), making them central examples in both nodes.
Laser welding is explicitly named in recent welding developments (B.4.g); laser cutting also relates to material removal by precision light-based machining (A.3).

Machines are the fundamental units of production processes, covering forming, material removal, and joining operations across section A; power trucks and mechanized conveyors also connect to materials-handling equipment (C.2), a priority uncovered path.

Robotics in manufacturing automates materials processing (A) and industrial materials handling, including conveyors, automated guided vehicles, and storage retrieval systems — directly relevant to the uncovered C paths on materials-handling systems and equipment.

Concrete production involves mixing and blending aggregates (A.1.a — mechanical processing) and casting molten/wet material into forms (A.2.a.ii — casting/molding nonmetals), making it a direct example of these production processes.

Electrometallurgy (B.2.b)—electrolytic smelting, electrowinning, and electrorefining — relies on electrical principles; electricity generation and distribution underpins industrial production processes throughout the section.

Gold refining is explicitly named in pyrometallurgical refining (B.2.a.iv) and electrorefining (B.2.b.iii); the article covers high-purity gold recovery through both thermal and electrochemical methods.

Mineral processing — crushing, grinding, and concentration of metallic minerals — is the first stage of metallurgy (B.1) and minerals as crystalline inorganic solids are the raw input for all subsequent extractive metallurgy operations.

Rocks are the primary material input to mineral processing (B.1: crushing and grinding of ores), which is the first step in extractive metallurgy; the article connects petrology to ore deposits and mining inputs.

Inorganic chemistry provides the theoretical basis for extractive metallurgy — redox reactions, coordination chemistry, and catalysis (B.2)—and for chemical processing operations including leaching, precipitation, and smelting (A.1.b).

Silicon production by electrolytic reduction of silica (analogous to the Hall-Héroult process, B.2.b.i) and silicon casting for semiconductor wafers (A.2.a.ii — molding nonmetals) relate to industrial production processes covered in the outline.
